title: Letters to the Editor
summary: In a brief press release issued in tandem with its first-quarter earnings release Monday, however, Lithia confirmed last week's newspaper report that the company plans to buy the Tony Chevrolet dealerships. Lithia, which has 80 stores in 12 Western states, has been on a growth tear since the company first sold stock to the public in 1996, when it had just five. It set its sights on Alaska in 2001, when it bought the Dodge and Chrysler dealerships in South Anchorage. Last year, it bought an Anchorage Hyundai dealership and a Chevrolet-Cadillac dealership in Fairbanks. Those four dealerships last year accounted for 4 percent of its total revenue of $2.5 billion last year, according to documents on file with the Securities and Exchange Commission. The Tony Chevrolet dealerships together will …

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: "I don't think it's fair to ask the community to assume ownership with the inherent financial obligation that's the direct result of a long time of no work or deferred maintenance by the state," Amylon said. Most state-owned harbors are managed by local governments, one reason the state wants to get out of the boat-slip business. Lawmakers poured millions of dollars into coastal communities during the 1990s to fund repairs and speed transfers. The latest effort was a statewide bond package approved by voters in 2002 that included $32 million to repair and transfer 15 harbors and floats to 10 coastal communities. Transportation Department deputy commissioner John MacKinnon said the state has no more money to help with repairs. "We're looking at a Chevrolet level, not a Cadillac level," MacK…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: Rick Koch, public works superintendent for the city of Palmer, said there are costs and benefits to each route. The most northerly route would avoid popular trails near Kepler-Bradley, but anything touching Crevasse Moraine, he said, "would be like whacking a bees' nest with a stick." Further complicating the northern route is the fact it would run near the borough landfill, which has plans to someday expand. The middle route might be the most cost effective, Koch said, but it not only cuts though trails but runs through private land owned by Anchorage Sand and Gravel. Koch said the most southerly route along the Glenn would cause the least amount of headache because it doesn't encroach on trails, but plans by the Department of Transportation to someday widen the highway complicate that op…

summary: Program takes in abuse victims' pets NO WAY OUT: Women may refuse to move to a shelter because of pets. By ROSE COX Anchorage Daily News (Published: July 22, 2004) This dog in sheriff's hat and denim was entered in the costume contest at the Dog Jog 2003. About 1,000 dogs and dog owners turn out every year for Friends of Pets' largest fund-raiser of the year, which produced $30,000 in 2003. Proceeds support the animal rescue group's Safe Haven for Pets Program, reduced-cost spay and neuter services, an emergency care program and animal rescues. title: Letters to the Editor
summary: Paperwork anchors Greenpeace ship VIOLATIONS: Fine possible over environmental concerns; captain OK with outcome. The Associated Press (Published: July 16, 2004) The Greenpeace ship Arctic Sunrise arrived in Ketchikan on Monday. Alaska officials say the vessel has broken state environmental regulations for not having a contingency plan for oil discharge prevention and not having obtained a state of Alaska certificate of financial responsibility. The ship, carrying activists through Southeast Alaska to protest logging in the Tongass National Forest, was ordered anchored until the violations are fixed. (Photo by TOM MILLER / Ketchikan Daily News via The Associated Press) Click on photo to enlarge KETCHIKAN -- State environmental officials say the Greenpeace ship Arctic Sunrise broke Alaska l…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: In June, 28,893 motorists came to Alaska, a 5 percent jump from last year. The arrivals are also stronger than in 2002. Ron Peck, executive director of the statewide tourism association, said his members are generally pretty happy with the way the summer is turning out. The numbers of cruise ship and airline travelers are also up, he said. "We're cautiously optimistic that the season will be as strong or stronger than 2001," Peck said. The last two tourism seasons in Alaska were down or flat, depending on the operator, because of economic and terrorism concerns after the terrorist attacks, travel experts say. People were sticking closer to home if they took vacations at all. Some tourism operators had expressed concern this spring that soaring prices at the pump might keep people away. But…

summary: The woman told an investigator that Fox started using her to get drugs in October 2002, according to Superior Court documents. Fox admitted to an investigator with the fraud control unit that he'd inappropriately prescribed drugs several times. The documents said some of those medications were paid for by Medicaid. The government insurance program for low-income people ended up paying for about $290 worth of prescriptions purchased in 2002 and 2003. The Superior Court ordered Fox to pay restitution to the patient and placed him on probation with the Department of Corrections. Reporter Ann Potempa can be reached at apotempa@adn.com or 257-4581. title: Letters to the Editor
summary: Dushkin was charged last Saturday in Unalaska District Court with seven counts of second-degree sexual abuse of a minor. Those charges accuse him of having sex several times with one girl, as recently as June 24, when she was 15 years old, and dating back to 2002. On Tuesday, he was charged with four additional sex offenses, including possession of child pornography, exploitation of a minor and two counts of third-degree sexual abuse of a minor. The latter two charges accuse Dushkin of having sexual contact with two girls, ages 13 and 14, in June or July 2003. The child pornography and exploitation charges involve a sex videotape of Dushkin, the 17-year-old girl and 31-year-old Misty King. King also has been charged with exploitation of a minor. title: Letters to the Editor
summary: She would not name the disease that killed him, but said he died in his sleep. Edwards, 38, was convicted in August 2002 of two counts of first-degree murder for the shooting deaths of his ex-wife, Mona Edwards, and her friend, Maela Crabtree, at the Culver Place home they shared near West High School. The Edwardses had been divorced for about a month. At his murder trial, the defense labeled the killings crimes of passion. Mona Edwards had gotten a domestic violence restraining order against Mark Edwards earlier that week. Prosecutors said Edwards planned the killings, ambushed Crabtree with a two-shot derringer at the front door, then executed Mona Edwards as she slept. Jurors took about two hours to convict him of two counts of first-degree murder. Superior Court Judge Elaine Andrews no…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: Planes join Alaska fire wars REPLACEMENTS: Three aircraft substitute for canceled tankers. The Associated Press (Published: June 17, 2004) A super scooper air tanker drops water on a smoldering hillside in La Canada Flintridge, Calif., in December 1999. Two CL-215 super scoopers, so called because they swoop down to collect water from nearby lakes, have been sent to Alaska. Joining them is a single-engine tanker known for accuracy in laying down retardant, usually to protect ground crews working near the flames. The three planes are stationed at the Alaska Fire Service base at Fort Wainwright. (Photo by KEVORK DJANSEZIAN / The Associated Press archive 1999) Click on photo to enlarge FAIRBANKS -- Three replacement airplanes so far have been effective in fighting fires in the state's wildlan…

The bank has offered an additional $30,000 contribution contingent upon the Salvation Army's raising matching funds from local credit unions. title: Letters to the Editor
summary: The upfront money required now makes it hard for the small-time commercial fishermen to get into business or weather a bad year, Templin said. Without the program, "the little guy eventually gets squeezed out, the little community eventually gets squeezed out," he said Tuesday. The idea behind the program is to increase IFQs held by local fishermen and strengthen local economies, said Phil Smith, a NOAA fisheries program administrator. "The feeling is this won't be a magic bullet," Smith said. "In those communities we're talking about, there is virtually no other economy than commercial fishing." Under the program, the nonprofit corporations would apply to NOAA to become a Community Quota Entity. The entity would buy and sell fishing quota for the community and would lease the quota to loc…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: It's a strong turnaround from 2003, when Koniag lost nearly $2 million on revenue of close to $70 million. Last year marked the company's first loss in nearly 20 years. The red ink stemmed partly from a stock market performance the company's annual report describes as "disastrous." Koniag's stocks and bonds lost $3.4 million in 2003. In the three prior years, the portfolio produced annual income averaging just over $3.3 million. The company also blamed its poor 2003 performance on Clarus Technologies, a Bellingham, Wash.-based subsidiary that makes environmentally friendly equipment to process industrial fluids. Clarus lost $1.2 million. Koniag had anticipated that the military would be a strong customer of Clarus' products, Anderson said. For a variety of reasons, it didn't happen. Also, …

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: The move surprised some residents who have visited village clinics for years. "We're 160 miles away from Fairbanks," said Cathy Zeitler, a Manley Hot Springs resident. "If you're not in an extreme emergency, that's where we have to go." Indian Health Service clinics often provide the best, or only, available professional health care in Alaska villages. Few of the villages are solely populated by Native Alaskans, and most are accessible only by airplane or riverboat. Clinics have routinely looked the other way, according to the memo written by Suzanne McKechnie, the conference's Community Health Aide director. Among villages receiving the memo were Huslia, Koyukuk, Kaltag, Dot Lake, Northway and Tanacross. The memo was posted on the door of the Manley clinic, Zeiter said. In response, a fli…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: Companies bid $53.9 million on Alaska petroleum reserve leases By MATT VOLZ, Associated Press Writer ANCHORAGE, Alaska (June 2, 1:12 pm ADT) - Five oil companies on Wednesday bid for the right to develop 1.4 million acres in the National Petroleum Reserve-Alaska, in what was the state's largest onshore federal lease sale. The high bids totaled $53.9 million and covered 123 individual tracts of land. The highest bid for a single tract was $13.74 million, made by Fortuna Energy Inc., a subsidiary of Calgary, Alberta-based Talisman Energy Inc. The land, more than 5 million acres in the northwest region of the NPR-A, is remote and without infrastructure. It could take more than a decade to explore and develop, said officials with the federal Bureau of Land Management. "The energy resources of …

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: Petroleum advocate urges changes to attract investment The Associated Press KENAI (June 2, 1:55 am ADT) - Alaska remains the most costly place on the planet to produce oil and gas but the state could take steps to attract major oil company investment, according to the head of the Alaska Oil and Gas Association. Judy Brady spoke Tuesday to the Kenai chapter of The Alliance, a trade organization representing nearly 400 businesses, organizations and individuals that derive their livelihood from providing products and services to oil and other natural resource companies. Brady based her remarks in part on a 2002 study by Wood Mackenzie of Edinburgh, Scotland, an international energy consulting firm. The firm is working on an update for 2004 that could change Alaska's ranking. Two years ago, Al…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: More cases of suspected virus reported in Fairbanks The Associated Press FAIRBANKS (May 4, 9:00 am ADT) - State public health officials say they have found 12 more suspected cases of norovirus, bringing the total number to 30 since last week. Health officials said they found the additional cases last week while tracking down possible victims after the first 18 cases surfaced. Officials said they believe none of the newly identified victims were suffering new symptoms. Dr. Marc Chimonas, a state medical epidemiologist with the Alaska Department of Health, said the two businesses where the cases are believed to have originated have been cleared by public officials. Chimonas declined to name the businesses but identified them as a hotel and a day-care center. Conclusive identification of a no…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: The jolt, capable of severe damage had it occurred near populated areas on land, did not generate a tsunami, Knight said. While the West Coast and Alaska Tsunami Warning Center in Palmer estimated the magnitude at 7.0, the National Earthquake Information Center in Golden, Colo., put it at 6.7. Both numbers were preliminary. Gary Neilson of Meyers Chuck, 40 miles north of Ketchikan, said he was awakened around 2 a.m. "The bed was shaking pretty good," Neilson said. The observatory received numerous calls from people who felt the quake in Craig and Petersburg but had no reports of significant damage -- "just items falling off the shelves," Knight said. title: Create your eCard The Anchorage Fire Department is capitalizing this week on the planned demolition of the old Dimond Hi…
summary: Create your eCard The Anchorage Fire Department is capitalizing this week on the planned demolition of the old Dimond High School on Arlene Street to get some realistic training. AFD spokesman Tom Kempton said firefighters from across town will be practicing search and rescues and other tactics they might have to use during a fire, such as forcing down doors, breaking windows and punching through roofs. The school is scheduled for demolition next week. Kempton said firefighters don't get to do this type of training very often. "It's expensive and destructive," he said. "To have a big commercial roof like this -- we don't ever get a chance to see it unless it's the real thing. It's a tremendous asset." Above: Senior fire captain John Adamson, in red helmet, gives commands to his Dimond Stat…

John M. Knauss puts a copy of the funeral service for Floyd Mack Jr. in his pocket during his sentencing for second-degree murder and first-degree assault in Palmer Superior Court on Monday. title: Letters to the Editor
summary: Ruedrich, state enter settlement over ethics charges By DAN JOLING, Associated Press Writer ANCHORAGE, Alaska (June 22, 11:00 pm ADT) - The state has reached a settlement with GOP Chairman Randy Ruedrich on charges that he violated state ethics law while working as a member of the Alaska Oil and Gas Conservation Commission. Ruedrich admitted to three violations of the state Ethics Act and has been fined $12,000 - believed to the largest civil penalty ever imposed in a state ethics case, according to the attorney general's office. Chief Assistant Attorney General Barbara Ritchie, who headed the investigation, said her office had two requirements for settlement: that Ruedrich admit his conduct violated the Ethics Act and that he pay substantial civil penalties. "We negotiated a settlement th…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: "Some good will come out of this," Maloney said. Representatives from the Fairbanks North Star Borough School District said the conference solidified their decision to retrain their staff members in a different approach to bullying prevention. And Noe, after advice from a speaker, said she may decide to transfer her son to another school. "I'm the only one who's stood up for him," Noe said. "Kids are downright mean." Why are some kids mean? Why are others targeted? And how can schools work to prevent it? The conference's experts offered up research and suggestions. Bullying is sometimes a product of "a natural but nasty social process," said Shelley Hymel, an education professor at the University of British Columbia. Put a group of people together, Hymel said, and there's a predictable res…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: Sen. Con Bunde, R-Anchorage, asked Murkowski what his reaction would be if lawmakers approved a statute -- not requiring a public vote -- that would accomplish most of what the constitutional proposal would. Bunde suggested such a plan would include earnings from the $27.8 billion fund. "Clearly my reaction would be you've got this authority," Murkowski replied. "That's a legislative right." Murkowski said he's always felt if lawmakers are going to change the fund itself, that would require a vote of the people, but if they were going to use the authority they have always had to spend its earnings on government, that would be their right. In the past Murkowski has pledged not to spend earnings from the oil-wealth savings account without a vote of the people. Lawmakers legally can spend ear…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: Pat Joensuu, timber manager for Silver Bay Logging in Wrangell, said while his cutters aren't particularly happy about having to roll out of bed four hours earlier than normal, they're also pragmatic. "Oh, they mind," Joensuu said. "But it's part of the deal when you're in this business." Log hauling, loading and road-building is not subject to hoot-owl rules, so that work can continue throughout the day. Fire danger throughout Southeast ranges from high to very high, according to the Forest Service. On Sunday, the agency classified the danger as extreme in the Thorne Bay area of Prince of Wales Island, near Ketchikan. The 17-million-acre Tongass is a temperate rain forest that is usually downright soggy. Average rainfall in Ketchikan, one of the bigger communities in the Tongass, is 155 i…
